questions.prompt: Why use Turborepo (or Nx) with a monorepo?

choices.text: Task orchestration with caching and affected-by-changes detection. Without it: every PR runs every test/lint/build for every package — slow and wasteful. With Turborepo: tasks run in dependency order; outputs cache; unchanged packages skip rebuild. `turbo run test --filter=...[main]` runs only packages affected by changes since main. CI scales with what changed, not with repo size. Remote caching shares builds across team and CI — local dev uses CI's cache instead of rebuilding. As the monorepo grows past ~5 packages, Turborepo/Nx becomes essential; otherwise CI becomes painful.

questions.explanation: Task orchestration + caching + affected detection. CI scales with changes, not repo size.

questions.prompt: Monorepo has 12 packages but each push runs all tests for all packages — 20 minutes CI. Fix?

choices.text: Use affected-by-changes detection via Turborepo's `--filter=...[main]` (or Nx's `nx affected`). The tool examines the dependency graph and determines which packages depend (directly or transitively) on changed files; runs tests only for affected packages. A change to a leaf package may affect 1-2 things; a change to a deeply-shared utility may affect 10. Either way, you only test what's affected. Combine with task caching so even affected packages skip work if their inputs haven't changed. 20-minute CI typically drops to 2-5 minutes for typical PRs. Standard pattern for any non-trivial monorepo.

questions.explanation: Use Turborepo --filter=...[main] or Nx affected. Test only packages affected by changes.
